coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescence and UV–vis absorption dual-modal sensors for Ag+ and l-cysteine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Shan
Li, Zixuan
Fu, Xueyu
Xie, Zhigang
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: Although a multitude of carbon dots (CDs) based sensors have spring up, most of them are fluorescent probes. Other optical properties, such as optical absorption has been neglected for too long. In order to improve reliability of results, making use of fluorescence (FL)/UV–Vis absorption dual signaling probes is an effective approach. Herein, orange emissive CDs were synthesized and utilized as FL/UV–vis dual-functional probes for the first time, to selectively detect Ag+ and l-Cysteine (L-Cys), with low limit of detection of 0.05 μM and 0.02 μM. In addition, CDs have the advantages including unique stability, good biocompatibility and high sensitivity, which demonstrate excellent performance in selectively detecting intracellular changes of Ag+ and L-Cys. This work provides a feasible way to construct multifunctional biosensors for the potential applications in environmental survey and biological assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
UV–Vis absorption
Dual signaling probes
Ag+ and l-Cysteine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The selectivity of CDs for Ag+ detection
Sensitivity of CDs for Ag+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution, (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum, (d) FL spectra, (e) FTIR spectrum, (f) XPS survey spectrum, (g) C1s, (h) N1s, (i) O1s high-resolution XPS spectra of CDs.
(a) FL changes of CDs vs pH (λex = 400 nm). (b) Relation ship between F–F0 vs pH. (c) FL changes of CDs under illumination. (d) FL changes of CDs toward NaCl.

(a) Photographs of CDs solution mixing with metal ions (UV lamp, 365 nm). (b) FL spectra of CDs with metal ions. (c) FL response of CDs toward Ag+ and various metal ions (2 Ag+, 3 Li+, 4 Na+, 5 K+, 6 Mg2+, 7 Mn2+, 8 Ni2+, 9 Pb2+, 10 Cd2+, 11 Ca2+, 12 Ce2+, 13 Co2+, 14 Zn2+, 15 Cu2+, 16 Fe2+), F/F0 is the maximum FL intensity of CDs with and without metal ions, λex = 400 nm, [CDs] = 50 μg mL−1, [metal ions] = 500 μM.

(a) FL changes of CDs (50 μg mL−1) vs Ag+ (0–800 μM). (b) The linear relationship between (F0–F) and [Ag+] (0–8 μM) (F0 and F is the FL intensity of CDs at 550 nm without and with Ag+, respectively). (c) UV–vis absorption changes of CDs vs Ag+. (d) Relationship between the absorbance of CDs and [Ag+], where A0 and A is the absorbance of CDs at 217 nm without and with Ag+.

(a) FL changes of CD-Ag vs L-Cys (0–180 μM). (b) Linear relationship of (F–F0) vs L-Cys concentration (5–25 μM). F0 and F is the FL intensity of CD-Ag at maximum emission peak without and with L-Cys, respectively). (c) UV–vis absorption changes of CD-Ag vs L-Cys. (d) Relationship between the absorbance of CD-Ag and L-Cys concentration,where A0 and A is the absorbance of CD-Ag at 293 nm without and with L-Cys, respectively.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images of L929 cells. (a) Incubated with CDs before and (b,c) after the addition of Ag+ (10 and 20 μM). (d,e) Cultured with CDs and Ag+ (20 μM) after adding L-Cys (50 and 150 μM). Scale bar is 20 μm.
The synthetic route of CDs, and CDs are used as FL/UV–Vis absorption dual signaling probes for Ag+ and L-Cys detection.
Detection of Ag+ by using CDs in lake water.
Detection of L-Cys by using CD-Ag in lake water.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescence and UV–vis absorption dual-modal sensors for Ag+ and l-cysteine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although a multitude of carbon dots (CDs) based sensors have spring up, most of them are fluorescent probes. Other optical properties, such as optical absorption has been neglected for too long. In order to improve reliability of results, making use of fluorescence (FL)/UV–Vis absorption dual signaling probes is an effective approach. Herein, orange emissive CDs were synthesized and utilized as FL/UV–vis dual-functional probes for the first time, to selectively detect Ag+ and l-Cysteine (L-Cys), with low limit of detection of 0.05 μM and 0.02 μM. In addition, CDs have the advantages including unique stability, good biocompatibility and high sensitivity, which demonstrate excellent performance in selectively detecting intracellular changes of Ag+ and L-Cys. This work provides a feasible way to construct multifunctional biosensors for the potential applications in environmental survey and biological assay.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver ions (Ag+) has received renewed attention because of the severe threats to environment and human health [1–4]. Recent researches have shown that excess Ag+ can produce obvious toxicity to cells, causing membrane damage and significant lysis of erythrocytes [5]. L-Cys has extensive involvement in physiological activities [6,7]. Abnormal L-Cys level can lead to growth retardation, skin lesions and liver injury [8–10]. In view of the importance of Ag+ and L-Cys, exploiting effective detection methods is highly desired.

Various techniques for detecting Ag+ and L-Cys have been developed, such as mass spectrometry [11], atomic absorption spectrometry [12,13], and electrochemical method [14] etc. Recently, considerable efforts have been devoted to exploit optical probes due to the simplicity, low cost, fast response and high sensitivity.

Carbon Dots (CDs) [15–21] with the merits of excellent luminescence, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and high photostability, are regarded as one of the first choice for sensing. CDs have been developed as a unique sensing platform for probing anions [22–24], cations [25–28], organic pollutants [29,30] and bioactive substances [31–34]. However, almost all of them are FL sensors by monitoring photoluminescence changes [35,36]. Their optical absorption properties are often overlooked for a long time. For the purpose of improving the accuracy of results, utilizing FL/UV–Vis absorption dual signaling sensors is an alternative way, which allows mutual correction through two different signaling reporters on the same target analyte and makes the results more reliable. Nevertheless, as far as we know, dual signaling sensors for Ag+ and L-Cys are still very rare.

Herein, orange emissive CDs were synthesized from 1,2,4,5-benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride (BTT) and poly(2-ethyl-2-oxazoline) (PEtOx) via solvothermal method. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of CDs is 3.5%. Ag+ can coordinate with CDs to form CD-Ag complex, causing the FL and UV–vis spectra of CDs change ratiometricly (Scheme 1 ). Thus, we demonstrate the proof of concept of utilizing CDs as a FL/UV–vis dual-functional sensor for selectively probing Ag+ with extremely low LOD to be 0.05 μM. Since L-Cys has high affinity with Ag+ and it can remove Ag+ from the surface of CDs, which can not only effectively recover the FL of CDs but also enhance the UV–vis absorption of CDs. Therefore, CD-Ag is able to detect L-Cys through FL/UV–vis dual-mode with LOD of 0.02 μM. Moreover, CDs are successfully utilized as a visual bioprobing platform for sensing Ag+ and L-Cys in vitro.

100 mg of PEtOx and 50 mg of BTT were dissolved in ethanol (10 mL) to form a transparent solution. The solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooled down to room temperature naturally, the solvent was removed by rotary evaporator. The crude product was washed by ethyl acetate and stored at 4 °C.

To investigate the selectivity of CDs on Ag+, CDs (50 μg mL−1) was mixed with a series of metal ions (500 μM) (Ag+, Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Ce2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+ and Fe2+), respectively. The FL of the CDs-metal mixture was recorded by FL spectrophotometer under the excitation of 400 nm.

Mixture of CDs (50 μg mL−1) and different concentrations of Ag+ (from 1 to 800 μM) were prepared in Milli-Q water. The FL and UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on LS-55 fluorophotometer and Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer, respectively.

Different concentrations of L-Cys (1–120 μM) was added to the mixture of CDs (50 μg mL−1) and Ag+ (500 μM). The FL and UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on LS-55 fluorophotometer and Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer, respectively.

CDs were prepared via solvothermal method using PEtOx and BTT as precursors. CDs are uniformly spherical particles (Fig. 1 a), with the average size of 2.5 nm (Fig. 1b). CDs have one typical absorption peak at 293 nm (Fig. 1c) and maximum emission at 550 nm (λex = 400 nm) (Fig. 1d). The absolute FL quantum yield of CDs is 3.5%. The characteristic peaks of C–O, C–C, C–N, CO, CC, C–H and O–H are found in the FTIR spectrum of CDs, at 1031, 1250, 1415, 1479, 1626, 2947 and 3437 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1e). Three main peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s are observed in the full survey XPS spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1f). The XPS spectrum of the C1s was decomposed into three peaks at 287.1, 285.2 and 284.3 eV, corresponding to CO, C–N and C–C/CC, respectively (Fig. 1g). N1s spectrum displays the peaks of C–N and N–H, which located at 400.6 and 399.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 1h). In addition (Fig. 1i), O1s spectrum reveals the presence of C–O (534.3 eV) and O–H (532.1 eV).

To investigate the effect of pH on the luminescence of CDs, we prepared citric acid and disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solutions (pH 4.0–8.0, Table S1). FL intensity of CDs enhances gradually as pH rises from 4.0 to 8.0, implying the pH sensitivity of CDs (Fig. 2 a and b). All of the subsequent experiments were carried out in pH 7.0 solutions. FL intensity of CDs display negligible changes even after 3 h of continuous irradiation (Fig. 2c), indicating that CDs possess high photostability. Furthermore, FL intensity changes of CDs toward NaCl were monitored. As depicted in Fig. 2d, no obvious FL changes are observed even NaCl concentration is up to 1.5 M, confirming that CDs still maintain excellent stability in high ionic strength conditions. These results prove the feasibility of applying CDs as sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of CDs for Ag+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Various metal ions (Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Ce2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+ and Fe2+, 500 μM) were added to CDs solution (50 μg mL−1), respectively. When Ag+ was added, CDs FL was significantly quenched in a moment (Fig. 3 a), by contrast, other metals had negligible influence on CDs luminescence. FL spectra of CDs in the presence of diverse metal ions are displayed in Fig. 3b, only Ag+ can lead to a very noticeable FL decrease of CDs, indicating that CDs have high selectivity on Ag+. Cu2+ masking agent potassium pyrophosphate was added in order to eliminate the interference of Cu2+ ions in the detection of Ag+ [37]. In order to illustrate the specify of CDs to Ag+, we performed the interference experiments of metal ions on CDs. When metal ions were added to the mixture of CDs and Ag+ (CD-Ag), the relative FL intensity (F/F0) of CD-Ag remain nearly constant (Fig. 3c and Fig. S1), demonstrating that CDs possess strong anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of CDs for Ag+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: FL spectra of CDs versus Ag+ concentration were recorded (Fig. 4 a) and the FL fades gradually with increasing Ag+ concentration. Fig. 4b displays the FL change (F0–F) progressively enhances and then becomes flat when Ag+ concentration is over 200 μM. (F0–F) and Ag+ concentration (0–8 μM) has a good linear correlation (F0–F = 52.435[Ag+] + 10.781, R2 = 0.99) and the LOD is 0.05 μM. Simultaneously, the higher the Ag+ concentration, the fainter the CDs luminescence, which can be observed conveniently with naked eyes under UV lamp. The FL of CDs is almost completely quenched, as Ag+ concentration is 500 μM (Fig. S2a).

UV–vis assays of CDs toward Ag+ were performed too. As depicted in Fig. 4c, with Ag+ concentration increasing from 0 to 800 μM, a new peak at 217 nm appears and intensifies gradually. In the range of 10–180 μM, a linear regression equation is obtained to be (A–A0) = 0.00476[Ag+] - 0.00269, R2 = 0.98 and the LOD is 0.46 μM (Fig. 4d). Based on the above results, CDs can act as a FL/UV–vis dual-modal sensor for Ag+ detection. The phenomenon that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs changes after the addition of Ag+, confirms that the quenching of the CD-Ag system is triggered by complex formation of Ag+ and CDs [38]. The LODs of other recently reported Ag+ probes are summarized in Table S2 and CDs obtained herein have the highest sensitivity among the probes.

When 200 μM of various biothiols such as Malic acid (MLA), Methionine (Met), Glutathione (GSH), 3,3′-Dithiodipropionic acid (DPA), d-cysteine (D-Cys), l-cysteine (L-Cys) were introduced into CD-Ag system, L-Cys can significantly restore the FL of CDs (Fig. S3), demonstrating that CD-Ag has a specific recognition to L-Cys. This is because plentiful sulfhydryl groups and amino groups on the surface of cysteine can effectively coordinate with Ag [39].

The fluorescent titration experimets were carried out by adding diverse concentrations of L-Cys to CD-Ag (Ag+:500 μM, CDs:50 μg mL−1). The FL of CDs was restored gradually with the addition of L-Cys (Fig. 5 a). Fig. 5b shows the relationship between (F–F0) of CD-Ag and L-Cys concentration, a linear correlation (F–F0 = 2.2123 [L-Cys] + 9.486) was achieved in the range of 5–25 μM, R2 = 0.99. LOD for L-Cys was estimated to be 0.02 μM. Photographs of CDs solutions recovered by L-Cys can be observed under UV lamp irradiation (Fig. S2b). In addition, the absorption band at around 293 nm enhanced steadily upon the addition of L-Cys. An excellent linear correlation between [L-Cys] and the absorbance of CD-Ag was regressed to be (A–A0) = 0.0034 [L-Cys]-0.010, R2 = 0.99. The LOD is 0.82 μM (Fig. 5c and d). Table S3 summarizes the previous reports on sensing L-Cys by fluorescent probes, the LOD is in the range from 0.045 to 2.03 μM. Therefore, L-Cys can be quantitatively detected according to the changes in FL and UV absorption intensities of CD-Ag.

Since D-Cys and GSH can also restore the FL of CDs (Fig. S3), CD-Ag was utilized for sensing D-Cys and GSH. The FL of CDs was restored gradually with the addition of D-Cys and GSH (Figs. S4a and S5a). Fig. S4b shows the relationship between (F–F0) of CD-Ag and D-Cys concentration, a linear correlation (F–F0 = 1.2788 [D-Cys] −0.1932) was achieved in the range of 0–12 μM, R2 = 0.98. Fig. S5b shows the relationship between (F–F0) of CD-Ag and GSH concentration, a linear correlation (F–F0 = 0.1865 [GSH] + 5.1787) was achieved in the range of 6–40 μM, R2 = 0.99. LOD for D-Cys and GSH was estimated to be 0.07 μM (Figs. S4a and b) and 7.09 μM (Figs. S5a and b), respectively. In addition, the absorption band at around 293 nm enhanced steadily upon the addition of D-Cys and GSH (Figs. S4c and S5c). An excellent linear correlation between [D-Cys] and the absorbance of CD-Ag was regressed to be (A–A0) = 0.0046 [D-Cys] + 0.0293, R2 = 0.95. The LOD is 0.88 μM (Figs. S4c and d). As shown in Figs. S5d and a good linear correlation ((A–A0) = 0.0030 [GSH] + 0.0051) over the range from 0 to 70 μM (R2 = 0.96) was obtained. The LOD was calculated to be 1.29 μM. Thus, CD-Ag exhibits highest sensitivity on L-Cys among the three types of biothiols (Table S4).

In order to verify the real application of CDs for the assay of Ag+, the detection of Ag+ in lake water was performed and the results were shown in Table 1 . The recovery varied between 91.7% and 142.6% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 11.26. In addition, CD-Ag was used to detect L-Cys in the lake water, and the results are shown in Table 2 . The RSD was lower than 5.64 and the recoveries were found to be varied between 91.8% and 158.5%. These results demonstrated that CDs could probe Ag+ and L-Cys in environmental water sample.

In general, FL can be quenched in two ways: static quenching effect (SQE) and dynamic quenching effect (DQE). To investigate the quenching mechanism of CDs for Ag+ sensing, further experiments were performed. The FL decay curves of CDs in the presence of Ag+ and L-Cys were investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, The average FL lifetime (τave) was calculated according to the following formula:
τave = (A1τ1 2 + A2τ2 2)/(A1τ1 + A2τ2)

The τave of CDs was calculated to be 14.78 ns, and the FL lifetime is significantly reduced to 6.16 ns after the addition of Ag+. When L-Cys was added to CD-Ag system, due to the stronger chelation of L-Cys and Ag+, Ag+ can be competitively trapped by L-Cys and removed away from CDs surface, leading to the luminescence recovery of CDs. The FL lifetime of CD-Ag-L-Cys is 7.66 ns.

To investigate the feasibility of CDs for cellular bioimaging, we evaluated biocompatibility of CDs by MTT assay. The high viability of L929 cells indicates that CDs have low cytotoxicity (Fig. S7). As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, after incubated with CDs (50 μg mL−1) for 24 h, L929 cells can emit intense red FL under the excitation of 555 nm laser. In contrast, when L929 cells were further treated with Ag+ (10 μM) for 0.5 h, weak FL was found (Fig. 6b). Moreover, no observable FL was observed when the Ag+ concentration reached to20 μM (Fig. 6c). Conversely, the red FL of CDs was recovered bit by bit with the addition of L-Cys (Fig. 6d and e). The results mentioned above verify that CDs can monitor intracellular Ag+ and L-Cys fluctuations.

We developed FL and UV absorption dual signaling probes based on CDs. CDs can sense Ag+ ions with the LOD of 0.05 μM. Furthermore, CD-Ag complex can selectively detect L-Cys, with the LOD of 0.02 μM. Given the good biocompatibility, high photostability and ionic strength stability, CDs can be utilized for sensing intracellular Ag+ and L-Cys. Our work opens up promising perspectives for developing CDs-based FL/UV absorption dual-signal probes for environmental monitoring and biological analysis.

Shan Lu: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Zhigang Xie: Writing - review & editing. Min Zheng: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Research Project of Science and Technology of the Education Department of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20200648KJ). We acknowledge the help of Prof. Ning Zhang from Northeast Normal University.

Kang, Carbon dots derived from citric acid and glutathione as a high-efficient intracellular ROS scavenger for alleviating the lipopolysaccharide induced inflammation in macrophage, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces (2020), DOI:10.1021/acsami.0c11735.
